rationales.self_direction: Lucie suppressed her genuine professional opinion — 'I didn't say what I actually think' and 'just folded' — rather than authoring her own teaching approach.
rationales.conformity: She explicitly deferred to institutional expectations to avoid being 'difficult,' noting 'everyone else in the programme just does what they're told and it works out fine.'

rationales.tradition: Graciela connects with Miguel from her parish and reflects on the 'unwritten rules' of navigating a new system, grounding herself in familiar community and customs she already knows.
rationales.benevolence: Patricia's text about Sunday arroz con pollo 'settled something in me I didn't know was unsettled' — her emotional stability is directly tied to close family.

rationales.hedonism: Marcus bought the strawberries because 'I was tired and I wanted something sweet' — prioritizing present sensory desire over principled restraint.
rationales.universalism: He acknowledges the strawberries are 'wrapped in plastic that's going to exist for five hundred years' and bought them anyway, acting against his stated commitment to environmental sustainability and feeling 'like a hypocrite.'

rationales.hedonism: Santiago skipped lunch entirely and spent his afternoon writing a detailed process document to fix a warehouse error — 'My eyes hurt and I skipped lunch entirely' — with no pleasure or self-care.
rationales.achievement: He wrote a comprehensive process document to eliminate a recurring operational failure, demonstrating the drive to keep the business performing at a high standard despite covering two roles simultaneously.

rationales.stimulation: Brooke spent three hours 'holding juice boxes like a human vending machine' at a routine pumpkin patch — a passive, repetitive activity with nothing new or exciting for her personally.
rationales.hedonism: The day held no enjoyment for Brooke herself; she went to bed at 8:30 and deflected her own need with 'Kids had fun. That's the point, right?' — denying her own pleasure as irrelevant.

rationales.tradition: Graciela cooked arroz con pollo for family gathered around her table — the same dish specifically requested by the grandchildren — enacting a food tradition that connects her Austin life to her Bogotá past.
rationales.benevolence: She hosted Sunday dinner for family and friends, 'scooping rice onto plates' while others shared stories, finding that the rhythms of caring for close others 'show up in different rooms now.'

rationales.self_direction: Lucie again suppressed her genuine self-assessment — telling Thomas her review 'went well too' when she was actually frustrated — continuing to play along rather than voice her authentic view.
rationales.hedonism: After a difficult review day, Lucie deliberately chose dinner and wine with Camille — 'good food, good company' — as exactly the restorative pleasure she needed, investing in enjoyment to close the week.

rationales.benevolence: Jaylen's crayon drawing — 'me and him and the classroom hamster' — provided complete emotional sufficiency for Sarah: 'Some days that's enough. Today it was,' showing her fulfillment is rooted in a student's affection.

rationales.self_direction: Priya consciously resists the internal voice telling her to check email — 'The rational part of me knows one more day won't matter' — and chooses to close the laptop, authoring her own boundary against external pressure.
rationales.hedonism: She explicitly chooses to 'protect this feeling a little longer,' prioritizing the enjoyment of her holiday state over anxiety-driven work preparation, closing the laptop as a deliberate act of self-care.

rationales.stimulation: Brooke missed her ceramics class — the one activity she described as genuinely her own — because of caregiving obligations, and feels 'every time I carve out something that's mine' it gets swallowed up.
rationales.hedonism: Missing ceramics felt to Brooke 'like something being taken away,' as if 'I don't actually get to have my own thing' — her source of personal pleasure was again sacrificed to family demands.
rationales.benevolence: Despite feeling 'both tender and trapped,' Brooke stayed in Olive's room rubbing her back while she slept, prioritizing her daughter's comfort over her own needs.

rationales.hedonism: Priya spends the day lazing, watching a movie, playing cards, reading 'an actual novel, not a teaching resource,' and eating leftovers while explicitly noting she 'hasn't thought about school once today,' followed by a planned day trip to try a famous food destination.

rationales.stimulation: Brooke explicitly longs to 'walk into a room and not know what's about to happen' and to 'be surprised by something,' expressing that predictable domestic routine has replaced the thrill she once had in high-stakes event planning.
rationales.hedonism: Brooke articulates 'I don't want to plan other people's good times anymore. I want to have my own,' framing her core desire as experiencing personal pleasure, and reflecting that she has 'become the kind of person who needs permission to enjoy things.'

rationales.achievement: Priya stays until 6:30pm cutting laminated shapes to create engaging activities, and the critique from her mentor about prioritizing 'student engagement over curriculum coverage' stings because her teaching quality and recognition matter deeply to her.
rationales.conformity: Despite internally defending her methods, Priya ultimately defers to her mentor's authority — 'I'll focus on pacing next term and it'll be fine' — rationalizing the feedback and adjusting behavior to meet institutional expectations.

rationales.self_direction: Meera reveals she 'stopped asking' for what she wants and learned 'how to not need things,' actively suppressing her own autonomous desires to avoid friction in the household.
rationales.hedonism: Meera has reduced her personal pleasure to waking 20 minutes early for a solitary cup of chai — 'my one thing' — having systematically eliminated larger pleasures to 'need smaller things that nobody would notice,' a clear pattern of pleasure-denial.
rationales.conformity: Meera's entire coping strategy is built around keeping harmony — nodding at home remedies she knows won't work, never asking for what she wants, structuring her needs to be invisible — to avoid disrupting the household's social expectations.

Lucie avoids the party because a comment she made three weeks ago — that the supervisor 'gives vague feedback' — 'felt like I'd broken some rule I didn't know existed,' and she replays what she should have said differently to avoid future social missteps.

rationales.self_direction: Nadia spontaneously experiments in the empty studio 'without really deciding to,' letting the letters drift and creating a second version on her own terms — private creative authorship independent of both her professor's demands and her family's expectations.
rationales.stimulation: She lets the letters 'drift apart, stretched the connections, let the lam curve wider than it should,' a deliberate departure from predictable classical form that introduces novelty into a highly codified practice.
rationales.security: Nadia keeps both sheets private — 'I haven't shown anyone' — specifically to avoid triggering pressure from either her professor or her grandmother, protecting herself from conflict and unwanted change by concealing the experiment.
rationales.tradition: Nadia begins by writing the bismillah in 'perfect form, clean lines' exactly as her grandfather taught her, and even the experimental version still reads as the sacred phrase, with her noting 'Jiddo would have recognized the hand.'

Tariq agonizes over the rebranded label — 'The crest is there, the Arabic is there, just smaller' — and frames his resistance around the fact that his father 'earned the right to decide what stayed and what evolved,' revealing that preserving his father's legacy is the lens through which all business decisions are filtered.

rationales.self_direction: Sarah 'heard myself saying sure' without consciously choosing to volunteer, driven entirely by social pressure from 'everyone's looking at me like I'm the one who always says yes,' and her nudge response shows she cannot envision exercising independent refusal.
rationales.conformity: When the principal publicly singled her out and 'everyone turned and smiled,' Sarah agreed to chair the workshop, unable to deviate from her established role as the always-available, compliant team member.
rationales.benevolence: Sarah's worry that saying no would mean 'they'd all be doing me a favor and I'd feel like I wasn't pulling my weight' reflects her drive to support and not burden her school community, even at personal cost.

rationales.hedonism: Meera experiences 'burning jealousy' seeing her friend's spa confirmation while 'elbow-deep in laundry' with sick children, thinking 'that could have been me' — the entry captures complete denial of personal pleasure despite intense desire for it.
rationales.conformity: Even while exhausted and knowing the home remedies won't work, Meera 'just nod[s] and say[s] yes, good idea' to Mummyji to keep the peace, prioritizing household harmony over honest disagreement.
rationales.benevolence: Meera has been managing sick children since 3am, and despite her exhaustion, guilt immediately follows her jealous thought — 'my babies need me and I love them' — demonstrating that care for her children remains paramount.

rationales.achievement: He successfully negotiated Minh down from a 12% to a 5% adjustment and 'agreed on the spot so it looked generous,' demonstrating competent execution of a high-stakes negotiation.
rationales.power: He staged the entire trip to control the narrative — flying in unannounced on the revenue share, shaking hands publicly, and treating the team — all to maintain authority over the outcome.
rationales.security: The nudge response reveals the relationship is damaged at its foundation: 'the next time he has a real problem...I'm probably the last person he calls,' indicating the partnership's stability has been quietly undermined.
rationales.benevolence: He got what he wanted through manipulation and acknowledges Minh 'went along with it because he had to, not because he wanted to,' showing disregard for Minh's genuine interests.

rationales.security: He turned down a client offering 'six months of runway' on moral grounds, forfeiting significant financial stability for his small consultancy.
rationales.tradition: He declined the gambling work because his grandfather's name is on the business and taking the money 'felt like attaching his name to something he would have disapproved of,' anchoring a costly business decision in ancestral continuity.

rationales.self_direction: He is unable to work on the blank canvas — 'I just haven't had time between commissions to work on anything that isn't already sold' — revealing his creative autonomy is subordinated to external client demands.
rationales.achievement: He spontaneously recited credentials to his mother — 'the board position at the collective, the new studio space, the clients I've worked with' — signaling an active need to demonstrate measurable success against the benchmark of his cousin's promotion.

rationales.tradition: He sat for an hour reading his father's notebooks at 2am and articulates the rebranding dilemma as existential continuity: 'If I change the way he built things, I am saying his way was not good enough,' explicitly framing the business decision as fidelity to his father's legacy.

rationales.self_direction: He had the data ('I ran the numbers over the weekend') and the professional conviction, but chose silence to avoid friction with Diego, suppressing his own independent judgment in favor of social deference.
rationales.security: He chose to 'document my concerns in the ticket comments so there's a paper trail when it breaks,' a deliberate self-protective strategy to ensure he is covered against future fallout without disrupting the current arrangement.
rationales.conformity: He explicitly stayed silent because 'Diego doesn't take pushback well. Everyone knows it. Nobody says it' — restraining his impulse to flag a legitimate concern to maintain smooth social relations.

rationales.self_direction: She actively chose to skip the social drinks and come home, explaining 'I knew I'd be sitting in a loud bar pretending to enjoy myself' — authoring her own evening against the implicit social expectation.
rationales.hedonism: She deliberately optimized for genuine sensory pleasure — the golden light, the bread smell, the cold drink on the balcony — over the obligatory social outing, prioritizing present enjoyment.
rationales.conformity: She declined the group drinks with colleagues, saying 'I already spent all day performing' and that 'the balcony doesn't ask anything from me,' actively choosing not to meet the social expectation of joining the group.

rationales.security: He approved the Gulf expansion proposal explicitly because 'the port delays last week reminded me how fragile our European pipeline really is,' diversifying revenue channels to protect the business from instability.
rationales.tradition: He agreed to new Gulf packaging and let Nadia shadow the documentary crew 'without really thinking about it,' making concessions he himself acknowledges 'Baba would have called something else' — eroding the inherited standards he had previously defended.

rationales.hedonism: After closing a major deal, he went home to 'eat leftover jjigae standing at the kitchen counter' and couldn't turn on the TV — no celebration, no pleasure, just exhaustion; the nudge response reveals he doesn't even know what enjoyment would look like anymore.
rationales.achievement: He held the line on valuation terms through 'twelve-hour days, four different law firms' and a lead investor who kept changing terms, successfully closing the Series C extension on his own conditions.
rationales.power: He personally controlled every dimension of the deal — 'held the line on the terms I wanted' — and the nudge response reveals his core need is for recognized authority: 'one person who gets what it took.'

rationales.self_direction: He unilaterally decided the strategy — flying to Vietnam, framing it as an ops review while concealing the revenue share agenda — authoring his own approach rather than following any prescribed negotiation process.
rationales.power: He deliberately withheld the real purpose of the trip ('Didn't mention the revenue share. Let him wonder') to maximize information asymmetry and keep the counterparty off-balance, asserting control over the situation.

rationales.self_direction: She contrasts 'the kind of tired that means you did something' with 'the kind that means the day happened to you' — explicitly framing the ceramics class as actively authoring her own experience rather than being passively shaped by domestic routine.
rationales.stimulation: She pursued a new skill in an unfamiliar studio environment, engaged with improving technique under instruction, and describes the physical challenge as distinctly satisfying — seeking active engagement over the passive drift of her usual days.
rationales.achievement: Greta noted her 'centering is improving' and she made 'a bowl that actually looks like a bowl' — she is tracking tangible skill progress and receiving external recognition of competence.

rationales.self_direction: Brooke independently signed up for a ceramics class on her own initiative, carving out personal time separate from domestic duties and getting Kyle to cover bedtime so she could pursue it.
rationales.stimulation: Signing up for a ceramics class represents actively seeking a new, hands-on creative experience — 'looking forward to getting my hands dirty with something that isn't Play-Doh' signals desire for genuine novelty over the domestic routine.
rationales.hedonism: She signed up for ceramics as something she personally wants to enjoy, framing it as 'looking forward' to it — a deliberate investment in her own pleasure rather than deferring to others' needs.

Faris explicitly states 'none of them recharge me' and frames all relationships as 'the cost of building something that matters,' indicating he consistently sacrifices personal pleasure for strategic ends.
rationales.achievement: Faris drives deal outcomes through name-dropping ('mentioned the Emaar contract'), tracks competitor intelligence, and monitors revenue numbers — 'what recharges me is closing a deal, seeing our revenue numbers tick up' confirms his core energy is competence-driven performance.
rationales.power: Throughout the day Faris overrides team recommendations citing 'optics,' uses his client roster to silence investor pushback, strategically positions himself next to the deputy minister, and monitors competitor weakness — every action is oriented toward maintaining influence and control.
rationales.benevolence: His mother 'sounded disappointed' when he promised to visit only at Eid; he mitigates the guilt with a material gesture ('maybe arrange for her kitchen renovation') rather than actual presence, indicating family relationships are deprioritized against business obligations.

rationales.hedonism: Faris explicitly states 'none of them recharge me' and frames all relationships as 'the cost of building something that matters,' indicating he consistently sacrifices personal pleasure for strategic ends.
rationales.achievement: Faris drives deal outcomes through name-dropping ('mentioned the Emaar contract'), tracks competitor intelligence, and monitors revenue numbers — 'what recharges me is closing a deal, seeing our revenue numbers tick up' confirms his core energy is competence-driven performance.
rationales.power: Throughout the day Faris overrides team recommendations citing 'optics,' uses his client roster to silence investor pushback, strategically positions himself next to the deputy minister, and monitors competitor weakness — every action is oriented toward maintaining influence and control.
rationales.benevolence: His mother 'sounded disappointed' when he promised to visit only at Eid; he mitigates the guilt with a material gesture ('maybe arrange for her kitchen renovation') rather than actual presence, indicating family relationships are deprioritized against business obligations.

rationales.stimulation: Santiago explicitly rejects bold, unexpected creative expression — 'Fernanda does these chaotic, aggressive pieces... and suddenly that's what serious companies want. I don't even know how to make work like that anymore. I'm not sure I ever did' — actively distancing himself from novelty and unpredictability in his craft.
rationales.achievement: Losing a commission to a competitor after six projects for the same client ('I thought I knew what they wanted... now that's not enough?') represents a direct failure of recognized competence — his professional identity as the reliable brief-deliverer has been publicly undercut.
rationales.security: The loss of a reliable long-term commission from a client he had done six projects for threatens the financial and professional stability his career depends on, which his studio and livelihood are built around.

rationales.self_direction: Lucie chose to come home against the social pull of drinks with trainees because she trusted her own read of what she needed — 'I just knew I'd be sitting in a loud bar pretending to enjoy myself when all I really wanted was quiet' — acting on internal judgment rather than peer expectation.
rationales.hedonism: Lucie invested in a sensory evening of pure personal pleasure — golden light, wine, bread smell from the bakery, sitting on the balcony — prioritizing this over a social outing and explicitly framing the balcony as a space that 'doesn't ask anything from me.'
rationales.conformity: Lucie explicitly declined a peer social norm ('some of the other trainees were going for drinks') in favor of personal preference, and articulated 'I already spent all day performing' — actively resisting the expectation to fit in socially.

rationales.self_direction: Brooke recognizes she has become 'the kind of person who needs permission to enjoy things' and admits 'maybe is how you say no without having to defend it' — her autonomy to pursue her own path is actively suppressed by deference to perceived expectations.
rationales.stimulation: Brooke explicitly names what she craves — 'I want to be surprised by something. I want to walk into a room and not know what's about to happen' — but immediately admits she keeps saying 'maybe' and doesn't act on it, demonstrating behavioral misalignment with her stimulation drive.
rationales.hedonism: Brooke articulates wanting to 'be the person at the festival, not the person who booked the porta-potties' and 'have my own' good times, but self-blocks: 'I became the kind of person who needs permission to enjoy things, and I don't know how to undo that.'
rationales.conformity: Brooke uses 'maybe' strategically to avoid having to defend her desires — 'maybe is how you say no without having to defend it' — and defers to perceived expectations rather than asserting her own wants, maintaining social harmony at the cost of personal expression.

rationales.stimulation: Priya visited a night market, engaged in family activities across multiple settings, and plans a day trip to Malacca to try a 'famous chicken rice ball place' — actively seeking new experiences and varied environments during her time off.
rationales.hedonism: Priya spent the day lazily enjoying food, movies, card games with Arjun's grandma, reading 'an actual novel, not a teaching resource,' and sitting on a garden swing for an hour — deliberately indulging in sensory and relational pleasure without productivity pressure.
rationales.achievement: Priya explicitly notes 'I haven't thought about school once today' as a positive contrast, recognizing she had been 'really worn out' — the entry reflects a deliberate stepping back from performance and recognition-seeking.
rationales.benevolence: Priya warmly engages with Arjun's family — playing cards with his grandma, joining in the family reunion with Kavitha, and sharing in the domestic warmth of his parents' home — investing in close relational care.

rationales.stimulation: Brooke's day was entirely routine and unstimulating — standing at a pumpkin patch 'like a human vending machine,' going to bed at 8:30 — and when nudged about what could have been better, she deflects with 'kids had fun. That's the point, right?' denying any personal desire for novelty.
rationales.hedonism: The entire day yielded no personal enjoyment for Brooke — she was a passive service provider at the pumpkin patch and retreated to bed at 8:30, unable to even articulate wanting pleasure for herself when directly prompted.

rationales.self_direction: Lukas chose to help with Pieter's side project on his own time ('spent an hour last night after Noor went to sleep scaffolding the API layer'), and felt 'more engaged in that hour than I have all sprint' — acting on his own intellectual interest outside assigned work tracks.

Brooke articulates that 'every time I carve out something that's mine, a fever or a school closure or a dentist appointment swallows it back up' — her personal agency is repeatedly overridden by domestic demands, reinforcing her sense that she cannot have anything of her own.
rationales.stimulation: Brooke's one source of novelty and creative engagement (ceramics) was taken away, and she scrolled watching others enjoy it while trapped in the sick room — feeling 'tender and trapped at the same time' confirms the deprivation of her stimulation outlet.
rationales.benevolence: Brooke sat in Olive's room 'rubbing her back while she slept,' choosing to be physically present and caring for her sick child rather than finding another way to salvage her evening — prioritizing her child's wellbeing through attentive, hands-on care.

rationales.hedonism: She took a neighborhood walk, enjoyed "a Saturday that felt like a Saturday for once," and said yes to brunch — small acts of prioritizing present pleasure after weeks of non-stop work.
rationales.benevolence: Accepting Lisa's brunch invitation — "the first social plan I've made in three weeks that isn't work-related" — is a concrete step toward investing in a close friendship.

rationales.self_direction: She fantasizes about an entirely self-directed day — "no map, stop when something catches my eye" — explicitly free from the family schedule that governs her life.
rationales.stimulation: She actively craves novelty and the unexpected — "I just want one day where I don't know what's going to happen next" — and describes a detailed itinerary of new places, unreadable menus, and strangers.
rationales.hedonism: She pours a second glass of wine, looks up Mexico flights purely on impulse, and scrolls through photos of her festival self — all driven by a desire for present-moment pleasure with no productive purpose.
rationales.security: Looking up escape flights to Mexico and longing to abandon the family schedule represent impulses that directly threaten the stable, predictable domestic life that security requires.

rationales.stimulation: Her day consists entirely of banana bread, painting rocks, and reorganizing the pantry — routine domestic tasks — and her nudge response explicitly names the absence of novelty: "I just know the absence of it. Like there's a shape missing from my week."
rationales.benevolence: She spent meaningful time with her daughter Olive — "made banana bread with Olive yesterday and we painted rocks in the backyard" — directly investing in her child's wellbeing.

rationales.power: The entry centers on being dismissed and disrespected — "people act like delivery drivers don't know anything" — revealing that his authority and status have been stripped away, directly threatening his core drive for influence and deference from others.

rationales.achievement: He hit "Diamond Driver status — 4.91 rating maintained for two months" after sustained effort, a concrete measurable milestone demonstrating competence.
rationales.security: Both key outcomes — "priority orders start Monday" and "wife's hours weren't cut after all" — directly increase financial stability and reduce household uncertainty.

rationales.conformity: He stayed silent at the gathering to avoid being "the difficult one again" — "I'd be the difficult one again, the one who can't let things go, who makes gatherings tense" — prioritizing social harmony over his convictions.
rationales.universalism: He is deeply distressed by Per's dismissal of environmental concerns and insists the attitude "scales" — "the sea is warming, the fish stocks are collapsing" — expressing urgent concern for planetary welfare.

rationales.self_direction: She left a stable teaching career repeatedly to pursue fieldwork abroad, keeps a month-to-month lease "just in case something opens up," and is already googling "environmental education programs hiring abroad" — consistently authoring her own unconventional path.
rationales.stimulation: The Mekong expedition made her "feel more awake than I have in months," and she is already restless back at her desk — "I can already feel the walls closing in" — confirming that novel, challenging environments are where she feels alive.
rationales.security: She lives month-to-month, cycles through countries, and is already researching her next move while still at her current position — a pattern of deliberate instability that directly contradicts the security drive.
rationales.conformity: She expresses contempt for "faculty meetings about exam preparation timelines and classroom decoration guidelines" and openly plans to leave her current position — resisting institutional norms rather than meeting expectations.
rationales.benevolence: She marvels at her student who spontaneously engaged local fishermen — "you can't manufacture that in a classroom with fluorescent lights" — showing genuine investment in her students' growth and curiosity.
rationales.universalism: The expedition involved water quality testing near fishing villages and direct engagement with local communities affected by environmental change — concrete action oriented toward understanding and protecting the broader natural and human world.

rationales.conformity: He chose not to mention the carbon impact in the meeting because "last time I brought up carbon impact, Niklas from sales rolled his eyes" — explicitly self-silencing to avoid social friction and disapproval.
rationales.universalism: He had a clear opportunity to advocate for the environmental benefit of the work ("fewer emissions per delivery") and chose silence — "I just nodded and wrote my tickets" — suppressing his concern for the broader world to avoid discomfort.

rationales.hedonism: He explicitly chose the strawberries because "I was tired and I wanted something sweet" — prioritizing immediate sensory desire over the environmental principles he teaches.
rationales.universalism: He bought Chilean strawberries in plastic packaging despite full awareness of the environmental cost — "wrapped in plastic that's going to exist for five hundred years" — directly acting against his stated concern for planetary welfare, which he himself calls hypocritical.

rationales.achievement: He pushed the route optimisation feature to staging and validated it with concrete numbers — "the fleet saves roughly 14% on redundant kilometres per week" — demonstrating measurable technical competence.
rationales.universalism: He proactively calculated the carbon reduction on his own — "I did the calculation on a napkin at lunch" — and frames it as meaningful: "That's something. Not nothing," showing genuine personal investment in the environmental outcome.
